title: Lingua franca

infobox.value: ([/ˌlɪŋɡwə ˈfræŋkə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); lit. 'Frankish tongue'; for plurals see [§ Usage notes](#Usage_notes)), also known as a **bridge language**, **common language**, **trade language**, **auxiliary language**, **link language** or **language of wider communication** (**LWC**), is a [language](/wiki/Natural_language "Natural language") systematically used to make communication possible between groups of people who do not share a [native language](/wiki/First_language "First language") or dialect, particularly when it is a third language that is distinct from both of the speakers' native languages.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Language used to facilitate communication between groups without a common native language

For other uses, see [Lingua franca (disambiguation)](/wiki/Lingua_franca_(disambiguation) "Lingua franca (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [French language](/wiki/French_language "French language").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Linguafranca.jpg/250px-Linguafranca.jpg)](/wiki/File:Linguafranca.jpg)

French dictionary printed in 1830 detailing the [Mediterranean Lingua Franca](/wiki/Mediterranean_Lingua_Franca "Mediterranean Lingua Franca")

A **lingua franca** ([/ˌlɪŋɡwə ˈfræŋkə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); lit. 'Frankish tongue'; for plurals see [§ Usage notes](#Usage_notes)), also known as a **bridge language**, **common language**, **trade language**, **auxiliary language**, **link language** or **language of wider communication** (**LWC**), is a [language](/wiki/Natural_language "Natural language") systematically used to make communication possible between groups of people who do not share a [native language](/wiki/First_language "First language") or dialect, particularly when it is a third language that is distinct from both of the speakers' native languages.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Lingua francas have developed around the world throughout human history, sometimes for commercial reasons (so-called "trade languages" facilitated trade), but also for cultural, religious, diplomatic and administrative convenience, and as a means of exchanging information between scientists and other scholars of different nationalities.[[2]](#cite_note-Nye-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Gordin-3) The term is taken from the medieval [Mediterranean Lingua Franca](/wiki/Mediterranean_Lingua_Franca "Mediterranean Lingua Franca"), a [Romance](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages")-based [pidgin language](/wiki/Pidgin_language "Pidgin language") used especially by traders in the [Mediterranean Basin](/wiki/Mediterranean_Basin "Mediterranean Basin") from the 11th to the 19th centuries.[[4]](#cite_note-4) A [world language](/wiki/World_language "World language")—a language spoken internationally and by many people—is a language that may function as a global lingua franca.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/The_Harmsworth_atlas_and_Gazetter_1908_Trade_language_world_map.jpg/330px-The_Harmsworth_atlas_and_Gazetter_1908_Trade_language_world_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Harmsworth_atlas_and_Gazetter_1908_Trade_language_world_map.jpg)

Trade languages of the world in 1908 from *The Harmsworth Atlas and Gazetteer*

Any language regularly used for communication between people who do not share a native language is a lingua franca.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Lingua franca is a functional term, independent of any linguistic history or language structure.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Pidgins](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin") are therefore lingua francas; [creoles](/wiki/Creole_languages "Creole languages") and arguably [mixed languages](/wiki/Mixed_language "Mixed language") may similarly be used for communication between language groups. However, the term *lingua franca* is equally applicable to a non-creole language native to one nation, often a colonial power, learned as a [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") and used for communication between diverse language communities in a colony or former colony.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

Lingua francas are often pre-existing languages with native speakers, but they can also be pidgins or creoles developed for that specific region or context. Pidgins are rapidly developed and simplified combinations of two or more established languages, while creoles are generally viewed as pidgins that have evolved into fully complex languages in the course of adaptation by subsequent generations.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Often, pre-existing lingua francas such as French are used to facilitate intercommunication in large-scale trade or political matters, while pidgins and creoles often arise out of colonial situations and a specific need for communication between colonists and indigenous peoples.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Pre-existing lingua francas are generally widespread, highly developed languages with many native speakers.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Conversely, pidgins are very simplified means of communication, containing loose structuring, few grammatical rules, and possessing few or no native speakers. [Creole](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language") languages are more developed than their ancestral pidgins, utilizing more complex structure, grammar, and vocabulary, as well as having substantial communities of native speakers.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Whereas a [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") language is the native language of a specific geographical community,[[13]](#cite_note-13) a lingua franca is used beyond the boundaries of its original community, for trade, religious, political, or academic reasons.[[14]](#cite_note-:4-14) For example, [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") is a *vernacular* in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") but it is used as a *lingua franca* in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), alongside [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_language "Filipino language"). Likewise, [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") serve similar purposes as industrial and educational lingua francas across regional and national boundaries.

Even though they are used as bridge languages, [international auxiliary languages](/wiki/International_auxiliary_language "International auxiliary language") such as [Esperanto](/wiki/Esperanto "Esperanto") have not had a great degree of adoption, so they are not described as lingua francas.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The term *lingua franca* derives from [Mediterranean Lingua Franca](/wiki/Mediterranean_Lingua_Franca "Mediterranean Lingua Franca") (also known as *Sabir*), the pidgin language that people around the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") and the eastern Mediterranean Sea used as the main language of commerce and diplomacy from the late [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") to the 18th century, most notably during the [Renaissance era](/wiki/Renaissance_era "Renaissance era").[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) During that period, a simplified version of mainly [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") in the eastern Mediterranean and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") in the western Mediterranean that incorporated many [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), and [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language") came to be widely used as the "lingua franca" of the region, although some scholars claim that the Mediterranean Lingua Franca was just poorly used Italian.[[14]](#cite_note-:4-14)

In Lingua Franca (the specific language), *lingua* is from the Italian for 'a language'. *Franca* is related to Greek Φρᾰ́γκοι (*[Phránkoi](/w/index.php?title=Phr%C3%A1nkoi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Phránkoi (page does not exist)")*) and Arabic إِفْرَنْجِي (*ʾifranjiyy*) as well as the equivalent Italian—in all three cases, the literal sense is '[Frankish](/wiki/Frankish_language "Frankish language")', leading to the direct translation: 'language of the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks")'. During the late [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), *Franks* was a term that applied to all Western Europeans.[[17]](#cite_note-HEL-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Through changes of the term in literature, *lingua franca* has come to be interpreted as a general term for pidgins, creoles, and some or all forms of vehicular languages. This transition in meaning has been attributed to the idea that pidgin languages only became widely known from the 16th century on due to European colonization of continents such as The Americas, Africa, and Asia. During this time, the need for a term to address these pidgin languages arose, hence the shift in the meaning of Lingua Franca from a single proper noun to a common noun encompassing a large class of pidgin languages.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

As recently as the late 20th century, some restricted the use of the generic term to mean only mixed languages that are used as vehicular languages, its original meaning.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Douglas Harper's *Online Etymology Dictionary* states that the term *Lingua Franca* (as the name of the particular language) was first recorded in English during the 1670s,[[23]](#cite_note-Harper-23) although an even earlier example of the use of it in English is attested from 1632, where it is also referred to as "Bastard Spanish".[[24]](#cite_note-Morgan-24)

## Usage notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Usage notes")]

The term is well established in its naturalization to English, so major dictionaries do not [italicize](/wiki/Italic_type "Italic type") it as a "foreign" term.[[25]](#cite_note-OxfordDictionaries-25)[[26]](#cite_note-AHD-26)[[27]](#cite_note-MW_Collegiate-27)

Its plurals in English are *lingua francas* and *linguae francae*,[[26]](#cite_note-AHD-26)[[27]](#cite_note-MW_Collegiate-27) with the former being the first[[26]](#cite_note-AHD-26)[[27]](#cite_note-MW_Collegiate-27) or only[[25]](#cite_note-OxfordDictionaries-25) form listed in major dictionaries, while the latter is rarely used.

### Historical lingua francas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Historical lingua francas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Ephesians_2%2C12_-_Greek_atheos.jpg/250px-Ephesians_2%2C12_-_Greek_atheos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ephesians_2,12_-_Greek_atheos.jpg)

[Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek")

The use of lingua francas has existed since antiquity.

[Akkadian](/wiki/Akkadian_language "Akkadian language") remained the common language of a large part of Western Asia from several earlier empires, until it was supplanted in this role by [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") historically served as a lingua franca throughout the majority of South Asia.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The Sanskrit language's historic presence is attested across a wide geography beyond South Asia. Inscriptions and literary evidence suggest that Sanskrit was already being adopted in Southeast Asia and Central Asia in the 1st millennium CE, through monks, religious pilgrims and merchants.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Until the early 20th century, [Literary Chinese](/wiki/Literary_Chinese "Literary Chinese") served as both the written lingua franca and the diplomatic language in East Asia, including China, [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"), [Ryūkyū](/wiki/Ry%C5%ABky%C5%AB_Kingdom "Ryūkyū Kingdom"), and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam").[[36]](#cite_note-36) In the early 20th century, [vernacular written Chinese](/wiki/Vernacular_written_Chinese "Vernacular written Chinese") replaced Classical Chinese within China as both the written and spoken lingua franca for speakers of different Chinese dialects, and because of the declining power and cultural influence of China in East Asia, English has since replaced Classical Chinese as the lingua franca in East Asia.

[Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") was the lingua franca of the Hellenistic culture. Koine Greek[[37]](#cite_note-collins-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-mw-39) (Modern [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): Ελληνιστική Κοινή, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Greek "Romanization of Greek"): *Ellinistikí Kiní*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Common Greek'; Greek: [[elinistiˈci ciˈni]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek")), also known as Alexandrian dialect, common Attic, Hellenistic, or Biblical Greek, was the [common supra-regional form](/wiki/Koin%C3%A9_language "Koiné language") of Greek spoken and written during the [Hellenistic period](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period"), the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") and the early [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"). It evolved from the spread of Greek following the conquests of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") in the fourth century BC, and served as the lingua franca of much of the Mediterranean region and the Middle East during the following centuries.[[40]](#cite_note-Bubenik-40)

[Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), through the power of the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), became the dominant language in [Italy](/wiki/Italy_(geographical_region) "Italy (geographical region)") and subsequently throughout the realms of the Roman Empire. Even after the [Fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire"), Latin was the common language of communication, science, and academia in Europe until well into the 18th century, when other regional vernaculars (including its own descendants, the Romance languages) supplanted it in common academic and political usage, and it eventually became a [dead language](/wiki/Dead_language "Dead language") in the modern linguistic definition.

[Old Tamil](/wiki/Old_Tamil "Old Tamil") was once the lingua franca for most of ancient [Tamilakam](/wiki/Tamilakam "Tamilakam") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"). [John Guy](/wiki/John_Guy_(historian) "John Guy (historian)") states that Tamil was also the lingua franca for early maritime traders from India.[[41]](#cite_note-scroll.in-41) The language and its dialects were used widely in the state of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") as the major language of administration, literature and common usage until the 12th century CE.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[Classical Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language") is the retrospective name for the language (formed out of many dialects, albeit all mutually intelligible)[[43]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Māori_language-43) of both the North Island and the South Island for the 800 years before the [European settlement of New Zealand](/wiki/Immigration_to_New_Zealand "Immigration to New Zealand").[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") shared a common language that was used for trade, inter-[iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") dialogue on [marae](/wiki/Marae "Marae"), and education through [wānanga](/wiki/W%C4%81nanga "Wānanga").[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) After the signing of the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi"), Māori language was the lingua franca of the [Colony of New Zealand](/wiki/Colony_of_New_Zealand "Colony of New Zealand") until English superseded it in the 1870s.[[43]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Māori_language-43)[[51]](#cite_note-:7-51) The description of Māori language as New Zealand's 19th-century lingua franca has been widely accepted.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[51]](#cite_note-:7-51)[[53]](#cite_note-auto-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) The language was initially vital for all European and [Chinese migrants](/wiki/Chinese_New_Zealanders "Chinese New Zealanders") in New Zealand to learn,[[55]](#cite_note-Revitalizing_Endangered_Languages-55)[[53]](#cite_note-auto-53)[[51]](#cite_note-:7-51) as Māori formed a majority of the population, owned nearly all the country's land and dominated the economy until the 1860s.[[55]](#cite_note-Revitalizing_Endangered_Languages-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) Discriminatory laws such as the [Native Schools Act 1867](/wiki/Native_schools "Native schools") contributed to the demise of Māori language as a lingua franca.[[43]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Māori_language-43) In earlier contact eras, Māori was also among the bases for [Maritime Polynesian Pidgin](/wiki/Maritime_Polynesian_Pidgin "Maritime Polynesian Pidgin") for use between European voyagers and trading Polynesians as a whole.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

[Sogdian](/wiki/Sogdian_language "Sogdian language") was used to facilitate trade between those who spoke different languages along the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"), which is why native speakers of Sogdian were employed as translators in [Tang China](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty").[[58]](#cite_note-58) The Sogdians also ended up circulating spiritual beliefs and texts, including those of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), thanks to their ability to communicate to many people in the region through their native language.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

[Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic"), an [Eastern South Slavic](/wiki/Eastern_South_Slavic "Eastern South Slavic") language, is the first Slavic [literary language](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language"). Between 9th and 11th century, it was the lingua franca of a great part of the predominantly [Slavic](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") states and populations in [Southeast](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), in [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") and church organization, culture, literature, education and diplomacy, as an [Official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") and [National language](/wiki/National_language "National language") in the case of [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"). It was the first national and also international Slavic literary language (autonym словѣ́ньскъ ѩꙁꙑ́къ, *slověnĭskŭ językŭ*).[[60]](#cite_note-lpd-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) The Glagolitic alphabet was originally used at both schools, though the [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") was developed early on at the [Preslav Literary School](/wiki/Preslav_Literary_School "Preslav Literary School"), where it superseded Glagolitic as the official script in [Bulgaria](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire") in 893. Old Church Slavonic spread to other South-Eastern, Central, and Eastern European Slavic territories, most notably [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"), [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"), [Lesser Poland](/wiki/Lesser_Poland "Lesser Poland"), and principalities of the [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'") while retaining characteristically [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavic_languages "South Slavic languages") linguistic features. It spread also to not completely Slavic territories between the [Carpathian Mountains](/wiki/Carpathian_Mountains "Carpathian Mountains"), the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"), corresponding to [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia") and [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia"). Nowadays, the Cyrillic [writing system](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") is used for various languages across Eurasia, and as the national script in various Slavic, [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages"), [Mongolic](/wiki/Mongolic_languages "Mongolic languages"), [Uralic](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages"), [Caucasian](/wiki/Caucasian_languages "Caucasian languages") and [Iranic](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages")-speaking countries in [Southeastern Europe](/wiki/Southeastern_Europe "Southeastern Europe"), Eastern Europe, the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"), Central, North, and East Asia.

The [Polish language](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") was a lingua franca,[[62]](#cite_note-BRILL-62)[[63]](#cite_note-Koyama_2007_137–153-63) important both diplomatically and academically in [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and part of [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"). [Tomasz Kamusella](/wiki/Tomasz_Kamusella "Tomasz Kamusella") notes that "Polish is the oldest, non-ecclesiastical, written Slavic language with a continuous tradition of literacy and official use, which has lasted unbroken from the 16th century to this day."[[64]](#cite_note-64) Polish evolved into the main [sociolect](/wiki/Sociolect "Sociolect") of the nobles in [Poland–Lithuania](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_union "Polish–Lithuanian union") in the 15th century.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamusella2009111-65) The history of Polish as a language of state governance begins in the 16th century in the [Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland"). Over the later centuries, Polish served as the official language in the [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"), [Congress Poland](/wiki/Congress_Poland "Congress Poland"), the [Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria"), and as the administrative language in the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire")'s [Western Krai](/wiki/Western_Krai "Western Krai"). The growth of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth")'s influence gave Polish the status of *lingua franca* in [Central and Eastern Europe](/wiki/Central_and_Eastern_Europe "Central and Eastern Europe").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamusella2009137-66)

The [Mediterranean Lingua Franca](/wiki/Mediterranean_Lingua_Franca "Mediterranean Lingua Franca") was largely based on Northern Italy's languages and secondarily on [Occitano-Romance languages](/wiki/Occitano-Romance_languages "Occitano-Romance languages"). This language was spoken from the 11th to 19th centuries around the Mediterranean basin, particularly in the European commercial empires of Italian cities ([Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), Venice, [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), Milan, [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa"), [Siena](/wiki/Siena "Siena")) and in trading ports located throughout the eastern Mediterranean rim.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

During the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), standard Italian was spoken as a language of culture in the main royal courts of Europe, and among intellectuals.[[68]](#cite_note-:1-68)[[69]](#cite_note-:5-69) Italian musical terms, in particular dynamic and tempo notations, have continued in use to the present day.[[70]](#cite_note-:6-70)[[71]](#cite_note-:8-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Hansesprache_no_text.jpg/250px-Hansesprache_no_text.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hansesprache_no_text.jpg)

Extent of Middle Low German in red with its use as a literary language black lines tilted right

[Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German"), also known as Low Saxon, used to be the Lingua franca during the late [Hohenstaufen](/wiki/Hohenstaufen "Hohenstaufen") till the mid-15th century periods ([Middle Low German](/wiki/Middle_Low_German "Middle Low German")), in the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") and the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") when extensive trading was done by the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") along the Baltic and North Seas.

[Classical Quechua](/wiki/Classical_Quechua "Classical Quechua") is either of two historical forms of [Quechua](/wiki/Quechuan_languages "Quechuan languages"), the exact relationship and degree of closeness between which is controversial, and which have sometimes been identified with each other.[[72]](#cite_note-72) These are:

1. the variety of Quechua that was used as a lingua franca and administrative language in the [Inca Empire](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire") (1438–1533)[[73]](#cite_note-snow,_stark1971-73) (or Inca lingua franca[[74]](#cite_note-74)). Since the Incas did not have writing, the evidence about the characteristics of this variety is scant and they have been a subject of significant disagreements.[[75]](#cite_note-75)
2. the variety of Quechua that was used in writing for religious and administrative purposes in the Andean territories of the Spanish Empire, mostly in the late 16th century and the first half of the 17th century and has sometimes been referred to, both historically and in academia, as *lengua general* ('common language')[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) (or Standard Colonial Quechua[[80]](#cite_note-80)).

[Ajem-Turkic](/wiki/Ajem-Turkic "Ajem-Turkic") functioned as lingua franca in the Caucasus region and in southeastern [Dagestan](/wiki/Dagestan "Dagestan"), and was widely spoken at the court and in the army of [Safavid Iran](/wiki/Safavid_Iran "Safavid Iran").[[81]](#cite_note-81)

#### English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: English")]

Main article: [English as a lingua franca](/wiki/English_as_a_lingua_franca "English as a lingua franca")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/English_language_distribution.svg/500px-English_language_distribution.svg.png)](/wiki/File:English_language_distribution.svg)

English language distribution

Majority native language

Official or administrative language, but not native language

English is sometimes described as the foremost global lingua franca, being used as a working language by individuals of diverse linguistic and cultural backgrounds in a variety of fields and international organizations to communicate with one another.[[82]](#cite_note-:3-82) English is the [most spoken language](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_total_number_of_speakers "List of languages by total number of speakers") in the world, primarily due to the historical global influence of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") as well as the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[83]](#cite_note-83) It is a [co-official language of the United Nations](/wiki/Official_languages_of_the_United_Nations "Official languages of the United Nations") and many other international and regional organizations and has also become the *de facto* language of [diplomacy](/wiki/Diplomacy "Diplomacy"), [science](/wiki/Science "Science"), [international trade](/wiki/International_trade "International trade"), [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), [aviation](/wiki/Aviation "Aviation"), [entertainment](/wiki/Entertainment "Entertainment") and the [Internet](/wiki/Languages_used_on_the_Internet "Languages used on the Internet").[[84]](#cite_note-84)

When the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") became a colonial power, English served as the lingua franca of the colonies of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). In the post-colonial period, most of the newly independent nations which had many [indigenous languages](/wiki/Indigenous_language "Indigenous language") opted to continue using English as one of their official languages such as [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana") and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[82]](#cite_note-:3-82) In other former colonies with several official languages such as [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") and [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), English is the primary medium of education and serves as the lingua franca among citizens.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

Even in countries not associated with the [English-speaking world](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world"), English has emerged as a lingua franca in certain situations where its use is perceived to be more efficient to communicate, especially among groups consisting of native speakers of many languages. In [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar"), the medical community is primarily made up of workers from countries without English as a native language. In medical practices and hospitals, nurses typically communicate with other professionals in English as a lingua franca.[[88]](#cite_note-melf-88) This occurrence has led to interest in researching the consequences of the medical community communicating in a lingua franca.[[88]](#cite_note-melf-88) English is also sometimes used in [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") between people who do not share one of Switzerland's [four official languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Switzerland "Languages of Switzerland"), or with foreigners who are not fluent in the local language.[[89]](#cite_note-89) In the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), the use of English as a lingua franca has led researchers to investigate whether a [Euro English](/wiki/Euro_English "Euro English") dialect has emerged.[[90]](#cite_note-90) In the fields of technology and science, English emerged as a lingua franca in the 20th century.[[91]](#cite_note-91) English has also significantly [influenced](/wiki/Englishisation "Englishisation") many other languages.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

#### Spanish

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Spanish")]

Main article: [Hispanophone](/wiki/Hispanophone "Hispanophone")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Map-Hispanophone_World.svg/500px-Map-Hispanophone_World.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map-Hispanophone_World.svg)

Spanish language distribution

Official language

Co-official language

Culturally important or secondary language (> 20% of the population)

The Spanish language spread mainly throughout the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World"), becoming a lingua franca in the territories and colonies of the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), which also included parts of Africa, Asia, and Oceania. After the breakup of much of the empire in the Americas, its function as a lingua franca was solidified by the governments of the newly independent nations of what is now [Hispanic America](/wiki/Hispanic_America "Hispanic America").[[93]](#cite_note-93) While its usage in Spain's Asia-Pacific colonies has largely died out except in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), where it is still spoken by a small minority, Spanish became the lingua franca of what is now [Equatorial Guinea](/wiki/Equatorial_Guinea "Equatorial Guinea"), being the main language of government and education and is spoken by the vast majority of the population.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

Due to large numbers of immigrants from Latin America in the second half of the 20th century and resulting influence, Spanish has also emerged somewhat as a lingua franca in parts of the [Southwestern United States](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States") and southern [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"), especially in communities where native Spanish speakers form the majority of the population.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)

Spanish is used as a lingua franca in Portuguese-speaking countries, mainly Portugal and Brazil, due to the [strong similarity between the two languages](/wiki/Comparison_of_Portuguese_and_Spanish "Comparison of Portuguese and Spanish"), especially in border areas, tourist destinations, and business settings.

At present it is the second most used language in international trade, and the third most used in politics, diplomacy and culture after English and French.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

It is also one of the most taught foreign languages throughout the world[[98]](#cite_note-98) and is also one of the [six official languages of the United Nations](/wiki/Official_languages_of_the_United_Nations "Official languages of the United Nations").

#### Italian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Italian")]

Main article: [Italian language](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Map_Italophone_World_-_updated.png/500px-Map_Italophone_World_-_updated.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Italophone_World_-_updated.png)

Italian language distribution

Areas where it is the majority language

Areas where it is a minority language or where it was a former colonial language

Areas where notable Italian-speaking communities are present

During the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), Italian, particularly the Tuscan variety that became the basis of standard Italian, was considered the lingua franca among the European elite and royal courts, especially in cultural and intellectual circles. The literary authority of figures such as [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch") and [Giovanni Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio") established Italian as a major language of humanist scholarship and literature. Moreover, the political and economic influence of Italian city-states like the [Republic of Florence](/wiki/Republic_of_Florence "Republic of Florence") and the [Republic of Venice](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") attracted diplomats, merchants, artists, and scholars from across Europe, many of whom engaged with Italian as a language of culture, commerce, and artistic exchange. While Latin remained the primary language of formal scholarship and diplomacy, Italian functioned as an important vernacular medium within elite artistic, literary, and courtly environments during that period.[[68]](#cite_note-:1-68)[[69]](#cite_note-:5-69)

From the 17th century onward, Italian became the principal lingua franca of [European music](/wiki/European_music "European music"), particularly in opera and classical composition. Because Italy was the birthplace of [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera") and a leading center of musical innovation, its terminology was adopted internationally. Composers such as [Claudio Monteverdi](/wiki/Claudio_Monteverdi "Claudio Monteverdi") and later [Antonio Vivaldi](/wiki/Antonio_Vivaldi "Antonio Vivaldi") helped solidify Italian’s prestige, and musical terms became standard across Europe.[[70]](#cite_note-:6-70)[[71]](#cite_note-:8-71)

Today, Italian is the main working language of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), serving as the lingua franca in the [Roman Catholic hierarchy](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_hierarchy "Roman Catholic hierarchy") and the official language of the [Sovereign Military Order of Malta](/wiki/Sovereign_Military_Order_of_Malta "Sovereign Military Order of Malta"). Italian influence led to the development of [derivated languages and dialects](#Influence_and_derived_languages) worldwide. It is also widespread in various sectors and markets, with [its loanwords](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_Italian_origin "List of English words of Italian origin") used in arts, luxury goods, fashion, sports and cuisine; it has a significant use in [musical terminology](/wiki/Musical_terminology "Musical terminology") and [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera"), with numerous Italian words referring to music that have become international terms taken into various languages worldwide, including [in English](/wiki/List_of_Italian_musical_terms_used_in_English "List of Italian musical terms used in English").[[99]](#cite_note-99)

Italian is an [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") ([Ticino](/wiki/Ticino "Ticino") and part of the [Grisons](/wiki/Italian_Grisons "Italian Grisons")), and [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"), and it has official [minority status](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") ([Istria](/wiki/Slovene_Istria "Slovene Istria")), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"),[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101) [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"),[[102]](#cite_note-coe-102) and in [6 municipalities of Brazil](/wiki/Languages_of_Brazil#Language_co-officialization "Languages of Brazil").[[103]](#cite_note-encantado-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) It is also spoken in other European and non-EU countries, most notably in [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") (by 66% of the population),[[105]](#cite_note-105) [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") (upwards of 70%),[[106]](#cite_note-Zonova,_Tatiana_2013-106) and [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco").[[107]](#cite_note-107)

#### French

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: French")]

Main article: [Francophonie](/wiki/Francophonie "Francophonie")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Map-Francophone_World.svg/500px-Map-Francophone_World.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map-Francophone_World.svg)

French language distribution

Majority native language

Official language, but not a majority native language

Administrative or cultural language

French is sometimes regarded as the first global lingua franca, having supplanted [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") as the prestige language of politics, trade, education, diplomacy, and military in [early modern](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") Europe and later spreading around the world with the establishment of the [French colonial empire](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire").[[108]](#cite_note-Wright-108) With [France](/wiki/France "France") emerging as the leading political, economic, and cultural power of Europe in the mid-17th century, the language was adopted by royal courts throughout the continent, including the United Kingdom, Sweden, and Russia, and as the language of communication between European academics, merchants, and diplomats.[[109]](#cite_note-109) With the expansion of Western colonial empires, French became the main language of diplomacy and international relations up until [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") when it was replaced by English due the rise of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") as the leading [superpower](/wiki/Superpower "Superpower"). Stanley Meisler of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* said that the fact that the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") was written in English as well as French was the "first diplomatic blow" against the language.[[110]](#cite_note-110) Nevertheless, it remains the second most used language in international affairs and is one of the [six official languages of the United Nations](/wiki/Official_languages_of_the_United_Nations "Official languages of the United Nations").[[111]](#cite_note-andaman.org-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113)

As a legacy of French and [Belgian](/wiki/Belgian_colonial_empire "Belgian colonial empire") colonial rule, most former colonies of these countries maintain French as an official language or lingua franca due to the many indigenous languages spoken in their territory. Notably, in most Francophone [West](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa") and [Central African](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa") countries, French has transitioned from being only a lingua franca to the native language among some communities, mostly in urban areas or among the elite class.[[114]](#cite_note-114) In other regions such as the French-speaking countries of the [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb") ([Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"), [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia"), [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), and [Mauritania](/wiki/Mauritania "Mauritania")) and parts of the [French Caribbean](/wiki/French_Caribbean "French Caribbean"), French is the lingua franca in professional sectors and education, even though it is not the native language of the majority.[[115]](#cite_note-Maamri1013-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117)

French continues to be used as a lingua franca in certain cultural fields such as [cuisine](/wiki/Cuisine "Cuisine"), [fashion](/wiki/Fashion "Fashion"), and [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport").[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[108]](#cite_note-Wright-108)

As a consequence of [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit"), French has been increasingly used as a lingua franca in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and its institutions either alongside or, at times, in place of English.[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120)

#### Chinese

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Map-Sinophone_World.png/330px-Map-Sinophone_World.png)](/wiki/File:Map-Sinophone_World.png)

Map of the [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese")-speaking world.

Today, [Standard Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") is the lingua franca of [China](/wiki/China "China") and [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), which are home to many mutually unintelligible [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") and, in the case of Taiwan, indigenous [Formosan languages](/wiki/Formosan_languages "Formosan languages"). Among many [Chinese diaspora](/wiki/Chinese_diaspora "Chinese diaspora") communities, [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") is often used as the lingua franca instead, particularly in Southeast Asia, due to a longer history of immigration and trade networks with southern China, although Mandarin has also been adopted in some circles since the 2000s.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

#### Arabic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Arabic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Arabic_Dispersion.svg/250px-Arabic_Dispersion.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Arabic_Dispersion.svg)

Arabic language map  
Dark green: majority; light green: significant minority

[Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") was used as a lingua franca across the Islamic empires, whose sizes necessitated a common language, and spread across the Arab and Muslim worlds.[[122]](#cite_note-122) In [Djibouti](/wiki/Djibouti "Djibouti") and parts of [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea"), both of which are countries where multiple official languages are spoken, Arabic has emerged as a lingua franca in part thanks to the population of the region being predominantly Muslim and Arabic playing a crucial role in Islam. In addition, after having fled from Eritrea due to [ongoing warfare](/wiki/Eritrean_War_of_Independence "Eritrean War of Independence") and gone to some of the nearby Arab countries, Eritrean emigrants are contributing to Arabic becoming a lingua franca in the region by coming back to their homelands having picked up the Arabic language.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

#### Russian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Russian")]

Main article: [Russophone](/wiki/Russophone "Russophone")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Idioma_ruso.PNG/250px-Idioma_ruso.PNG)](/wiki/File:Idioma_ruso.PNG)

Areas where Russian is the majority language (medium blue) or a minority language (light blue)

Russian is in use and widely understood in [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") and the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"), areas formerly part of the Russian Empire and Soviet Union. Its use remains prevalent in many [post-Soviet states](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states"). Russian has some presence as a minority language in the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states") and some other states in Eastern Europe, as well as in pre-[reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up") China.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It remains the official language of the [Commonwealth of Independent States](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States"). Russian is also one of the six official languages of the United Nations.[[124]](#cite_note-un.org-124) Since the [collapse of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), its use has declined in post-Soviet states. Parts of the Russian speaking minorities outside Russia have either emigrated to Russia or assimilated into their countries of residence by learning the local language, which they now prefer to use in daily communication.

For contrast, in [Central European](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") countries that after the Second World War were included in the Soviet Union's sphere of influence, the Russian language was used only as [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc")'s language of internal political communication. There are no Russian minorities in these countries, in schools the primary foreign language is English and nowadays the Russian language practically does not exist.

[German](/wiki/German_language "German language") is used as a lingua franca in Switzerland to some extent; however, English is generally preferred to avoid favoring it over the three other official languages. German remains a widely studied language in Central Europe and the Balkans, especially in [former Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia#Federal_units "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"). It is recognized as an official language in countries outside of Europe, specifically [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia"). German is also one of the [working languages](/wiki/Working_language "Working language") of the EU along with English and French, but it is used less in that role than the other two.

#### Polish

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Polish")]

Historically, the [Polish language](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") was a lingua franca,[[62]](#cite_note-BRILL-62)[[63]](#cite_note-Koyama_2007_137–153-63) important both diplomatically and academically in [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and part of [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"). It is currently an official language in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and a recognised minority language in [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"),[[125]](#cite_note-euro-charter-125) [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"),[[126]](#cite_note-126)
[Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"),[[125]](#cite_note-euro-charter-125) [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"),[[127]](#cite_note-127) [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"),[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129) [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"),[[130]](#cite_note-130) [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"),[[125]](#cite_note-euro-charter-125) [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine").[[125]](#cite_note-euro-charter-125)[[131]](#cite_note-131) In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Polish Americans](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans") number more than 11 million.

[Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") served as lingua franca in the Portuguese Empire, Africa, South America and Asia in the 15th and 16th centuries. When the Portuguese started exploring the seas of Africa, America, Asia and Oceania, they tried to communicate with the natives by mixing a Portuguese-influenced version of lingua franca with the local languages. When Dutch, English or French ships came to compete with the Portuguese, the crews tried to learn this "broken Portuguese". Through a process of change the lingua franca and Portuguese lexicon was replaced with the languages of the people in contact. Portuguese remains an important lingua franca in the [Portuguese-speaking African countries](/wiki/Portuguese-speaking_African_countries "Portuguese-speaking African countries"), [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor"), and to a certain extent in [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") where it is recognized as an official language alongside Chinese though in practice not commonly spoken. Portuguese and Spanish have a certain degree of [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") and [mixed languages](/wiki/Mixed_language "Mixed language") such as [Portuñol](/wiki/Portu%C3%B1ol "Portuñol") are used [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] to facilitate communication in areas like the border area between Brazil and Uruguay.

#### Hindustani

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Hindustani")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Hindi_belt.png/250px-Hindi_belt.png)](/wiki/File:Hindi_belt.png)

The **Hindi Belt** (red) is a linguistic region in India where [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") (based on [Dehlavi](/wiki/Old_Hindi "Old Hindi")) serves as the *lingua franca.*

The [Hindustani language](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"), with [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") as dual standard varieties, serves as the lingua franca of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and [Northern India](/wiki/Northern_India "Northern India").[[132]](#cite_note-siddiqi1994-132)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[133]](#cite_note-pulsipher2005-133)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Many Hindi-speaking North Indian states have adopted the [three-language formula](/wiki/Three-language_formula "Three-language formula") in which students are taught: "(a) Hindi (with Sanskrit as part of the composite course); (b) Any other modern Indian language including Urdu and (c) English or any other modern European language." The order in non-Hindi speaking states is: "(a) the major language of the state or region; (b) Hindi; (c) Any other modern Indian language including Urdu but excluding (a) and (b) above; and (d) English or any other modern European language."[[134]](#cite_note-nic-134) Hindi has also emerged as a lingua franca in [Arunachal Pradesh](/wiki/Arunachal_Pradesh "Arunachal Pradesh"), a linguistically diverse state in Northeast India.[[135]](#cite_note-135)[[136]](#cite_note-136) It is estimated that nine-tenths of the state's population knows Hindi.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

Urdu is the lingua franca of Pakistan and had gained significant influence amongst its people, administration and education. While it shares official status with English, Urdu is the preferred and dominant language used for inter-communication between different ethnic groups of Pakistan.[[138]](#cite_note-138)

#### Malay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Malay")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Malayophone_world.svg/250px-Malayophone_world.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Malayophone_world.svg)

Countries where pluricentric Malay is spoken, regardless of standard variety

[Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") is understood across a cultural region in Southeast Asia called the "[Malay world](/wiki/Malay_world "Malay world")" including [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, southern [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), and certain parts of the Philippines. It is [pluricentric](/wiki/Pluricentric_language "Pluricentric language"), with several nations codifying a local vernacular variety into several national literary standards:[[139]](#cite_note-139) Although [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language") has more native speakers, Indonesia uses a standardized form of [Riau](/wiki/Riau-Lingga_Sultanate "Riau-Lingga Sultanate") Malay as the basis for the national language "[Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language")." Bahasa Indonesia is the sole official language even though it is the mother tongue of [only 7%](/wiki/Languages_of_Indonesia "Languages of Indonesia") of Indonesians.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

#### Swahili

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Swahili")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Maeneo_penye_wasemaji_wa_Kiswahili.png/250px-Maeneo_penye_wasemaji_wa_Kiswahili.png)](/wiki/File:Maeneo_penye_wasemaji_wa_Kiswahili.png)

Geographic extent of Swahili. Dark green: native range. Medium green: official use. Light green: bilingual use but not official.

[Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language") developed as a lingua franca between several [Bantu](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages")-speaking tribal groups on the east coast of Africa with heavy influence from Arabic.[[141]](#cite_note-:2-141) The earliest examples of writing in Swahili are from 1711.[[142]](#cite_note-142) In the early 19th century the use of Swahili as a lingua franca moved inland with the Arabic ivory and slave traders. It was eventually adopted by Europeans as well during periods of colonization in the area. German colonizers used it as the language of administration in [German East Africa](/wiki/German_East_Africa "German East Africa"), later becoming [Tanganyika](/wiki/Tanganyika_(territory) "Tanganyika (territory)"), which influenced the choice to use it as a national language in what is now independent [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania").[[141]](#cite_note-:2-141) Swahili is currently one of the national languages and it is taught in schools and universities in several East African countries, thus prompting it to be regarded as a modern-day lingua franca by many people in the region. Several [Pan-African](/wiki/Pan-African "Pan-African") writers and politicians have unsuccessfully called for Swahili to become the lingua franca of Africa as a means of unifying the African continent and overcoming the legacy of colonialism.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

[Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), an [Iranian language](/wiki/Iranian_language "Iranian language"), is the official language of [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") ([Dari](/wiki/Dari_language "Dari language")) and [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan") ([Tajik](/wiki/Tajik_language "Tajik language")). It acts as a lingua franca in both Iran and Afghanistan between the various ethnic groups in those countries. The Persian language in South Asia, before the [British colonized the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), was the region's lingua franca and a widely used official language in north India and Pakistan.

#### Hausa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Hausa")]

[Hausa](/wiki/Hausa_language "Hausa language") is the language of communication between speakers of different languages in Northern [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") and other West African countries,[[144]](#cite_note-144) including the northern region of Ghana.[[145]](#cite_note-145)

#### Amharic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Amharic")]

[Amharic](/wiki/Amharic_language "Amharic language") is the lingua franca and most widely spoken language in Ethiopia, and is known by most people who speak another Ethiopian language.[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147)

Creoles, such as [Nigerian Pidgin](/wiki/Nigerian_Pidgin "Nigerian Pidgin") in Nigeria, [Haitian Creole](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole"), and [Patois](/wiki/Patois "Patois") etc. are used as lingua francas across the world. This is especially true in Africa, the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), [Melanesia](/wiki/Melanesia "Melanesia"), Southeast Asia and in parts of Australia by [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians").

#### Sign languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lingua_franca&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Sign languages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Sign_Languages_of_Turtle_Island.svg/250px-Sign_Languages_of_Turtle_Island.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Sign_Languages_of_Turtle_Island.svg)

Map of the various sign languages of North America, excluding [Francosign](/wiki/Francosign_languages "Francosign languages") languages. [Hand Talk](/wiki/Plains_Indian_Sign_Language "Plains Indian Sign Language") was the predominant lingua franca prior to European settlement, able to be written down and signed alongside oral languages

The majority of pre-colonial North American nations communicated internationally using [Hand Talk](/wiki/Plains_Indian_Sign_Language "Plains Indian Sign Language").[[148]](#cite_note-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149) Also called Prairie Sign Language, Plains Indian Sign Language, or First Nations Sign Language, this language functioned predominantly—and still continues to function[[150]](#cite_note-150)—as a second language within most of the (now historical) countries of the Great Plains, from [Newe Segobia](/wiki/Western_Shoshone "Western Shoshone") in the West to [Anishinaabewaki](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe") in the East, down into what are now the northern states of Mexico and up into [Cree](/wiki/Cree "Cree") Country stopping before [Denendeh](/wiki/Denendeh "Denendeh").[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152) The relationship remains unknown between Hand Talk and other manual Indigenous languages like [Keresan Sign Language](/wiki/Keresan_Sign_Language "Keresan Sign Language") and [Plateau Sign Language](/wiki/Plateau_Sign_Language "Plateau Sign Language"), the latter of which is now extinct (though Ktunaxa Sign Language is still used).[[153]](#cite_note-153) Although unrelated, perhaps [Inuit Sign Language](/wiki/Inuit_Sign_Language "Inuit Sign Language") played and continues to play a similar role across [Inuit Nunangat](/wiki/Inuit_Nunangat "Inuit Nunangat") and the various [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") [dialects](/wiki/Inuit_languages "Inuit languages"). The original Hand Talk is found across [Indian Country](/wiki/Indian_Country "Indian Country") in pockets, but it has also been employed to create new or revive old languages, such as with [Oneida Sign Language](/wiki/Oneida_Sign_Language "Oneida Sign Language").[[154]](#cite_note-154)

[International Sign](/wiki/International_Sign "International Sign"), though a pidgin language, is present at most significant international gatherings, from which interpretations of national [sign languages](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language") are given, such as in [LSF](/wiki/French_Sign_Language "French Sign Language"), [ASL](/wiki/American_Sign_Language "American Sign Language"), [BSL](/wiki/British_Sign_Language "British Sign Language"), [Libras](/wiki/Libras "Libras"), or [Auslan](/wiki/Auslan "Auslan"). International Sign, or IS and formerly Gestuno, interpreters can be found at many [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") parliamentary or committee sittings,[[155]](#cite_note-155) during certain United Nations affairs,[[156]](#cite_note-156) conducting international sporting events like the [Deaflympics](/wiki/Deaflympics "Deaflympics"), in all [World Federation of the Deaf](/wiki/World_Federation_of_the_Deaf "World Federation of the Deaf") functions, and across similar settings. The language has few set internal grammatical rules, instead co-opting national vocabularies of the speaker and audience, and modifying the words to bridge linguistic gaps, with heavy use of gestures and [classifiers](/wiki/Classifier_constructions_in_sign_languages "Classifier constructions in sign languages").[[157]](#cite_note-157)
